How to Add ESLint to a Vue Vite Project
Heads up: this post is based on ESLint 9.0+. Also note that
(mentioned later) hasn't been maintained for a while.
This post walks through adding ESLint to a Vue Vite project, using the Airbnb or Standard style guide.
First, create a Vue 3 Vite dev environment:
Follow the prompts to build out the Vue 3 + Vite project.
I'll demo with Vue 3, using Airbnb's style guide for this first environment.
For reference, here's the prompt sequence:

✔ Project name: … vue3-eslint-airbnb
✔ Add TypeScript? … No(✅) / Yes
✔ Add JSX Support? … No(✅) / Yes
✔ Add Vue Router for Single Page Application development? … No / Yes(✅)
✔ Add Pinia for state management? … No / Yes(✅)
✔ Add Vitest for Unit Testing? … No(✅) / Yes
✔ Add Cypress for both Unit and End-to-End testing? … No(✅) / Yes
✔ Add ESLint for code quality? … No / Yes(✅)
✔ Add Prettier for code formatting? … No(✅) / Yes
Quick note: I'm not installing Prettier because we'll use ESLint's rules for formatting. Installing both would mean two formatters with conflicting opinions, so we'll just stick with ESLint.
Before installing ESLint, let's look at what the project already has. Open
The default just enforces Vue 3's basic rules — Airbnb isn't included yet.
If you wanted Standard, you'd install
. This post focuses on Airbnb, so let's stick with
Pay attention to the order of
should come last so Vue 3 rules are applied first, then Airbnb on top. If you put
first, Airbnb rules would apply first and then conflict with Vue 3 rules, throwing errors.
You can verify this against how Vue CLI projects do it:
comes last for the same reason.
is Vue's tweaked version of the Airbnb rules. If you don't want to use
works, but you'll likely spend more time tuning rules.
ESLint is now set up. Test it by opening
— you should see lots of red "

Since Airbnb is strict, you'll also want
Without this plugin, code like this errors:
// Unable to resolve path to module '@/components/HelloWorld.vue'.eslint(import/no-unresolved)
with the alias map and extensions:
Reopen the file that was erroring — ESLint resolves the alias correctly now.
is required for the Airbnb config — it covers import-related rules like import order, file existence, and so on.

The plugin "eslint-plugin-import" was referenced from the config file in ".eslintrc.cjs » eslint-config-airbnb-base » /home/runner/work/project-name/project-name/node_modules/eslint-config-airbnb-base/rules/imports.js".
If you hit this kind of error, install

'vite' should be listed in the project's dependencies, not devDependencies. eslint(import/no-extraneous-dependencies)
'@vitejs/plugin-vue' should be listed in the project's dependencies, not devDependencies. eslint(import/no-extraneous-dependencies)
This one's odd because Vite's default setup puts these in
— that's intentional. We need to tell ESLint that these packages are allowed in
tells ESLint these packages are valid even when they live in
Show ESLint warnings in the browser
If you want ESLint warnings visible in the dev browser, install
Now ESLint warnings show up in the browser when there are errors:
On some Node versions you might hit:
[vite] Internal server error: Failed to load plugin 'n' declared in '.eslintrc.cjs': Cannot find module 'eslint-plugin-n'
[vite] Internal server error: Failed to load plugin
